quiz_questions.question_text: Approximately what percentage of global greenhouse gas emissions does the food system contribute?

options.text: About 50%
About 5%
About 10%
About 25%, from food production, land use change, transport, processing, and waste

quiz_questions.explanation: The global food system, including agriculture, land use change, transportation, processing, and waste, contributes approximately 25% of total greenhouse gas emissions, making it a major driver of climate change.

quiz_questions.question_text: What primarily causes oceanic dead zones?

options.text: Excess nitrogen and phosphorus from agricultural fertilizer runoff causing algal blooms and oxygen depletion
Overfishing that removes too many organisms from coastal waters
Natural seasonal temperature changes in the ocean
Only industrial chemical discharges from factories

quiz_questions.explanation: Dead zones are primarily caused by excess nitrogen and phosphorus from agricultural fertilizer runoff, which triggers massive algal blooms. When the algae die and decompose, the process consumes dissolved oxygen, creating hypoxic zones that suffocate marine life.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the Gulf of Mexico dead zone linked to?

options.text: Urban sewage discharge from coastal cities
Offshore oil drilling in the Gulf
Natural ocean current patterns that reduce oxygen levels
Fertilizer runoff from the Mississippi River watershed, which drains agricultural lands across the Midwest

quiz_questions.explanation: The Gulf of Mexico dead zone, one of the largest in the world, is primarily caused by nitrogen and phosphorus from agricultural fertilizers that wash off farmlands across the Midwest and flow down the Mississippi River into the Gulf.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does Integrated Pest Management (IPM) prioritize?

options.text: Biological controls, habitat manipulation, and resistant crop varieties first, with chemical pesticides used only as a last resort
Exclusively organic methods that prohibit all pest control chemicals
Genetically modified crops as the primary pest control strategy
Maximum application of chemical pesticides to eliminate all pests

quiz_questions.explanation: IPM uses a hierarchy of pest control methods, prioritizing biological controls (natural predators), habitat manipulation, and resistant varieties before resorting to chemical pesticides, which are used only when other methods are insufficient.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the primary purpose of planting cover crops?

options.text: To provide food for livestock during winter months
To prevent erosion, build soil organic matter, suppress weeds, and sequester carbon between cash crop seasons
To produce an additional cash crop for sale at market
To improve the appearance of farm fields during off-seasons

quiz_questions.explanation: Cover crops are planted between cash crop seasons to protect and improve soil by preventing erosion, building organic matter, suppressing weeds, fixing nitrogen (legumes), and sequestering atmospheric carbon.

quiz_questions.question_text: What are the benefits of no-till farming?

options.text: It increases soil erosion but saves time and fuel
Reduced soil erosion, maintained soil structure and carbon storage, less fuel use, and better water retention
It only saves farmers money on equipment costs
It reduces labor but has no environmental benefits

quiz_questions.explanation: No-till farming preserves soil structure by avoiding plowing, which reduces erosion, maintains soil carbon stocks, improves water infiltration and retention, reduces fuel consumption, and supports soil microbial communities.

quiz_questions.question_text: How does crop rotation improve agricultural sustainability?

options.text: It is only beneficial in tropical climates where year-round growing is possible
It reduces the amount of labor needed but does not affect soil health
It only adds variety to a farm's product line for market purposes
It breaks pest and disease cycles, improves soil nutrients through nitrogen-fixing legumes, and reduces the need for chemical inputs

quiz_questions.explanation: Crop rotation disrupts pest and disease life cycles by changing host plants, improves soil fertility when nitrogen-fixing legumes are included, and reduces dependence on synthetic fertilizers and pesticides.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is precision agriculture?

options.text: Using GPS, sensors, drones, and data analysis to apply water, fertilizer, and pesticides only where and when needed
A government certification program for precise measurement of crop yields
A type of organic farming that uses no technology
Hand-planting and hand-harvesting crops for maximum precision

quiz_questions.explanation: Precision agriculture uses technology including GPS, remote sensors, drones, and data analytics to optimize the application of inputs (water, fertilizer, pesticides) by targeting only the areas and times where they are actually needed.

quiz_questions.question_text: How does organic farming differ from conventional farming?

options.text: Organic farming prohibits synthetic pesticides and fertilizers, relying instead on biological pest control, composting, and crop rotation
They are essentially identical in their methods and outcomes
Organic farming uses more chemicals but applies them in different ways
Organic farming is simply a marketing label with no real differences in practice

quiz_questions.explanation: Organic farming is regulated by standards that prohibit synthetic pesticides and chemical fertilizers, instead using biological pest control, composting, crop rotation, and other natural methods to maintain productivity.

quiz_questions.question_text: Approximately what percentage of food produced globally is wasted?

options.text: About 10%
About 1%
About 30-40%, wasted at production, retail, and consumer levels
About 5%

quiz_questions.explanation: Approximately 30-40% of all food produced globally is wasted across the supply chain, from post-harvest losses in developing countries to consumer waste in developed nations, representing enormous wasted resources, energy, and emissions.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is polyculture?

options.text: Growing multiple crop species together to increase biodiversity, reduce pest outbreaks, improve soil health, and reduce the risk of total crop failure
A government program that subsidizes diverse farming operations
A type of animal husbandry that raises multiple livestock species
Growing the same crop variety across very large acreage for maximum efficiency

quiz_questions.explanation: Polyculture involves growing multiple crop species together in the same space, which mimics natural ecosystems, increases biodiversity, reduces vulnerability to pests, improves soil health, and lowers the risk of total crop failure.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is agroforestry?

options.text: Planting trees only on land that cannot be farmed
A type of industrial timber plantation
Integrating trees with agricultural crops and/or livestock to provide multiple benefits including carbon sequestration, biodiversity habitat, and additional income
Converting forests entirely into agricultural land

quiz_questions.explanation: Agroforestry deliberately combines trees with crops and/or livestock, providing multiple benefits including carbon sequestration, biodiversity habitat, windbreaks, erosion control, shade, and additional income from tree products.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is composting?

options.text: Burning organic waste to generate energy
Mixing chemical fertilizers into soil to increase nutrient levels
Burying organic waste deep underground to prevent contamination
The process of converting organic waste into nutrient-rich soil amendment that improves soil structure, adds nutrients, and diverts waste from landfills

quiz_questions.explanation: Composting is the biological decomposition of organic waste (food scraps, yard waste) into nutrient-rich humus that improves soil structure, adds essential nutrients, supports soil microorganisms, and diverts waste from landfills.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is aquaponics?

options.text: Growing crops exclusively in water without any other inputs
A method of irrigating crops using treated wastewater
A system that combines aquaculture (fish farming) with hydroponics (soilless plant growing) in a recirculating loop where fish waste feeds plants
Large-scale ocean farming of shellfish and seaweed

quiz_questions.explanation: Aquaponics is a closed-loop system that combines fish farming with soilless plant growing: fish waste provides nutrients for the plants, and the plants filter the water for the fish, creating an efficient integrated food production system.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why is reducing meat consumption considered an environmental strategy?

options.text: Livestock production requires far more land, water, and energy per calorie than plant foods and produces significant methane emissions
It has no meaningful environmental impact
It only benefits human health, not the environment
It only reduces water usage but has no effect on emissions or land use

quiz_questions.explanation: Livestock production is highly resource-intensive, requiring much more land, water, and energy per calorie compared to plant-based foods, while also producing significant methane (a potent greenhouse gas) through enteric fermentation.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is soil health and why is it fundamental to sustainability?

options.text: Healthy soil supports plant growth, filters water, stores carbon, cycles nutrients, and supports biodiversity, making it the foundation of food systems
Soil health only matters for growing ornamental plants and gardens
Soil health is irrelevant because modern agriculture uses synthetic inputs
Soil health only matters for a single growing season at a time

quiz_questions.explanation: Soil is a living ecosystem that provides essential functions: supporting plant growth, filtering and storing water, sequestering carbon, cycling nutrients, and hosting a vast community of organisms, making soil health fundamental to long-term food security.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the GMO debate in sustainable agriculture?

options.text: GMOs are only debated for their taste and nutritional value
Potential benefits like pest resistance and higher yields are weighed against concerns about biodiversity impacts, corporate seed control, and unintended ecological effects
There is no debate; GMOs are universally accepted as sustainable
All scientists agree that GMOs are harmful to the environment

quiz_questions.explanation: The GMO debate involves complex trade-offs: potential benefits include pest resistance and increased yields, while concerns include impacts on biodiversity, herbicide-resistant weeds, corporate control of seed supplies, and possible unintended ecological effects.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the concept of sustainable yield in agriculture?

options.text: Producing food at a rate that can be maintained indefinitely without degrading the soil, water, and ecosystem resources that farming depends on
Maximizing crop yield in a single season regardless of long-term consequences
The minimum amount of food needed to feed a country's population
Only applying to fisheries, not land-based agriculture

quiz_questions.explanation: Sustainable yield in agriculture means maintaining production levels that can continue indefinitely without degrading the natural resource base (soil fertility, water quality, biodiversity) on which farming depends.

quiz_questions.question_text: Approximately how many billion people will sustainable agriculture need to feed by 2050?

options.text: About 7 billion
About 5 billion
About 20 billion
About 10 billion, while using less land, water, and chemicals

quiz_questions.explanation: By 2050, the global population is projected to reach approximately 10 billion people, and sustainable agriculture must feed this population while simultaneously reducing environmental impacts, representing one of humanity's greatest challenges.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a food desert?

options.text: A region where food waste is deposited in landfills
An agricultural region affected by drought and desertification
A type of arid land where no crops can grow
An area, often in low-income urban neighborhoods, where residents lack access to affordable, nutritious food such as fresh fruits and vegetables

quiz_questions.explanation: Food deserts are areas, typically in low-income urban communities, where residents have limited access to affordable, healthy food options, contributing to nutritional inequities and representing a social sustainability challenge.

quiz_questions.question_text: A farmer in the Midwest notices declining yields despite increasing fertilizer application. Soil tests reveal low organic matter, compacted soil structure, and reduced microbial activity. A county extension agent recommends switching to cover crops, no-till practices, and crop rotation. How would these three practices work together to restore soil health?

options.text: Cover crops add organic matter and prevent erosion, no-till preserves soil structure and microbial communities, and crop rotation breaks pest cycles and adds nitrogen through legumes
They would only reduce the farmer's costs without affecting soil health
Only crop rotation would help; the other two practices are unnecessary
These practices would further reduce yields because they eliminate modern farming techniques

quiz_questions.explanation: These practices form an integrated soil health system: cover crops add organic matter and protect soil, no-till preserves soil structure and supports beneficial organisms, and crop rotation (especially with legumes) breaks pest cycles and naturally adds nitrogen.

quiz_questions.question_text: A large-scale cattle operation in South America is located on recently cleared rainforest land. The ranch produces beef exported to developed countries. An environmental assessment reveals the operation contributes to deforestation, methane emissions, water pollution from manure, and biodiversity loss. Which sustainability dimensions are being violated?

options.text: Only the environmental pillar because the operation is economically profitable
Only the social pillar because workers may not receive fair wages
No sustainability dimensions because beef production is a natural economic activity
All three pillars: environmental (deforestation, emissions, pollution), economic (unsustainable resource depletion), and social (displacement of indigenous communities and loss of ecosystem services)

quiz_questions.explanation: This operation violates all three sustainability pillars: environmental damage from deforestation and pollution, economic unsustainability from depleting natural capital, and social harm from displacing indigenous communities and destroying ecosystem services.

quiz_questions.question_text: A school cafeteria wastes 30% of its food daily. Students propose three solutions: composting food scraps, buying from local farms to reduce spoilage, and adjusting portion sizes based on actual consumption data. How do these solutions relate to the food waste hierarchy?

options.text: Composting is the best solution because it turns all waste into a useful product
Adjusting portions (prevention) is most effective, local sourcing (reduction) is next, and composting (recycling) is the last resort; the hierarchy prioritizes preventing waste over managing it
All three solutions are equally effective and interchangeable
These solutions are too small-scale to have any meaningful impact

quiz_questions.explanation: The food waste hierarchy prioritizes prevention (right-sizing portions) over reduction (local sourcing to minimize spoilage) over recycling (composting). Preventing waste is always more efficient than managing waste after it occurs.

quiz_questions.question_text: A region is experiencing a pest outbreak that threatens crop yields. A conventional farmer applies broad-spectrum pesticides immediately. A neighboring farm using IPM monitors pest populations, introduces natural predators, applies targeted biological controls, and only uses a narrow-spectrum pesticide on the most affected areas. What are the sustainability advantages of the IPM approach?

options.text: Broad-spectrum pesticides are always more effective at controlling pests
IPM reduces chemical use and cost, preserves beneficial insects, prevents pest resistance, protects water quality, and maintains ecosystem balance
IPM has no advantages over conventional pesticide application
IPM only saves money but has no environmental benefits

quiz_questions.explanation: The IPM approach maintains ecological balance by preserving beneficial predator insects, reduces chemical contamination of water and soil, prevents the development of pesticide-resistant pest populations, and lowers costs while achieving effective pest control.

quiz_questions.question_text: A city surrounded by farmland notices its tap water contains high nitrate levels, traced to fertilizer runoff from upstream farms. The farms use conventional practices with heavy synthetic fertilizer application. Downstream, the river feeding into a bay has created a dead zone. If the farms transitioned to precision agriculture, what specific improvements would be expected?

options.text: Precision agriculture would apply fertilizer only where and when needed, reducing total application, lowering runoff and nitrate contamination, and helping shrink the dead zone
Precision agriculture would only reduce costs for farmers without affecting water quality
No improvements because precision agriculture uses the same total amount of fertilizer
The dead zone would immediately disappear once precision agriculture was adopted

quiz_questions.explanation: Precision agriculture uses GPS, sensors, and data analysis to apply fertilizer only where needed, reducing total application and minimizing excess that runs off into waterways. This would lower nitrate contamination in tap water and reduce nutrient loading contributing to the dead zone.

quiz_questions.question_text: An urban community wants to establish a local food production system in an area with very limited space, no arable land, and limited water. They are considering an aquaponics facility in a former warehouse. What makes aquaponics particularly suited to this scenario?

options.text: Aquaponics requires large amounts of land and water, so it is not suited to this scenario
Aquaponics requires no soil, uses up to 90% less water than conventional farming through recirculation, can be set up indoors, and produces both fish and vegetables in a compact space
Aquaponics only works in tropical climates with abundant natural light
Aquaponics produces only ornamental plants, not food crops

quiz_questions.explanation: Aquaponics is ideal for space-limited urban settings because it requires no soil, recirculates water (using up to 90% less than field agriculture), can operate indoors with artificial lighting, and produces both protein (fish) and vegetables in a compact integrated system.

quiz_questions.question_text: A developing country relies heavily on monoculture coffee production for export income. A drought followed by a pest outbreak devastates the harvest, leaving farmers with no income and communities without food. An agricultural advisor recommends transitioning to agroforestry. How would agroforestry address the multiple vulnerabilities exposed by this crisis?

options.text: Agroforestry would only improve the appearance of the farms without addressing productivity issues
Agroforestry diversifies income through tree products, provides shade that moderates drought stress on coffee, supports pest-controlling biodiversity, and includes food crops for local consumption
Agroforestry is only useful for timber production, not food crops
Agroforestry would completely eliminate the coffee crop and leave farmers without their primary income

quiz_questions.explanation: Agroforestry addresses multiple vulnerabilities: tree products diversify income, tree shade reduces drought stress on coffee plants, biodiversity from diverse plantings supports natural pest control, and food crops grown alongside coffee provide food security during poor harvests.

quiz_questions.question_text: A suburban family wants to reduce their food-related environmental footprint. They currently eat meat daily, buy most food from a large grocery chain, and discard about 25% of their groceries. Which combination of changes would have the greatest environmental impact?

options.text: Only growing herbs in a windowsill garden
Reducing meat consumption (especially beef), minimizing food waste through meal planning, and composting unavoidable food scraps
Only buying food in glass containers instead of plastic
Only switching to organic versions of the same products

quiz_questions.explanation: Reducing meat consumption (especially beef, which has the highest resource and emission footprint), eliminating food waste (which wastes all the resources used in production), and composting scraps would produce the greatest combined reduction in environmental impact.

quiz_questions.question_text: A government is considering two agricultural policies: Policy A subsidizes synthetic fertilizer purchases, keeping food prices low. Policy B provides incentives for cover crops, crop rotation, and reduced fertilizer use, but food prices may increase slightly in the short term. Using a sustainability framework, which policy better serves long-term food security?

options.text: Policy A because cheap food is the only goal of agricultural policy
Neither policy would affect long-term food security
Policy B because it builds long-term soil health, reduces water pollution, and creates a more resilient food system even though it costs slightly more in the short term
Policy A is more sustainable because subsidies encourage maximum food production

quiz_questions.explanation: Policy B better serves long-term food security by building soil health, reducing water pollution, and creating resilience. Fertilizer subsidies (Policy A) encourage overuse, degrading soil and water quality over time, ultimately undermining the production base.

quiz_questions.question_text: A region with increasing population pressure is debating whether to expand agriculture into remaining natural grasslands or intensify production on existing farmland. An ecologist argues that converting the grasslands would destroy carbon stored in prairie soils and eliminate habitat for pollinators that also serve nearby farms. How does this scenario illustrate the tension between food production and ecosystem services?

options.text: Intensifying production on existing farmland would have identical environmental impacts to expansion

Converting grasslands provides short-term food production gains but destroys carbon stocks and pollinator habitat that support long-term agricultural productivity, illustrating why ecosystem services must be valued in agricultural decisions
Ecosystem services are irrelevant to agricultural productivity
There is no tension because more farmland always means more food production

quiz_questions.explanation: This illustrates a key tension: expanding agriculture into natural ecosystems provides short-term food gains but destroys valuable ecosystem services (carbon storage, pollination) that support long-term agricultural productivity, showing why sustainable intensification on existing land is often preferable.
